source: Invest Like the Best — Tech and consumer growth investing (EP.149)
summary: Autonomous vehicles will have larger economic impact on retail real estate and urban/suburban living patterns than on the core automotive industry.
summary_extended: Baker argues that the most significant economic impact of autonomous vehicles will not be felt within the automotive industry itself, but rather in retail real estate and urban/suburban living patterns. His reasoning is that AVs will make long suburban commutes tolerable — enabling passengers to sleep, work, or use their phones — which could reverse the urbanization trend that has driven demand for urban living over the past decade. He further contends that when combined with drone delivery, AVs will fundamentally shift the competitive economics between e-commerce and physical retail, potentially altering which retail formats survive. Baker frames this as a 'lateral thinking' investment thesis, suggesting that the real opportunities lie in second-order effects on real estate and retail rather than in direct automotive or AV technology plays. No specific investment vehicles or companies are named, and the view is presented speculatively with a long-term horizon.
detail: AVs will make long suburban commutes tolerable (sleep, use phone in car), potentially reversing urbanization trends. Combined with drone delivery, AVs will shift the economics between e-commerce and physical retail. Baker frames lateral thinking about AV impact as more important than direct automotive investment.
confidence_evidence: Presented as a structural view but framed speculatively ('I think,' 'going to'); no specific investment vehicle named.
quote_fragment: cities may have a bigger impact than within the core automotive industry
